figures.caption: Table 19: Scoring configuration used for the reported digital assistant results.

Figure 5: EP interrupting an in-progress checkpoint handling. At tick 2 the agent is handling checkpoint A with 18 ticks of work remaining and no active alarm. At tick 3 an alarm appears at checkpoint D with deadline 19; because EP can re-decide every tick, the upper level immediately switches the agent from HANDLE_CKPT to NAV_ALARM (the abandoned handling work is preserved as ||18). Ticks 4–6 show the agent advancing one cell per tick toward D while the alarm deadline counts down 18 → 17 → 16, leaving ample slack to resolve the alarm before it expires.
Table 13: Configuration for the state-level interruption experiment.
Table 23: Multi-dish results by case family. Cells report mean ± half-width of the bootstrap 95% confidence interval.

Figure 1: Comparison of interaction interfaces. POMDPs pair observations and actions at fixed decision epochs; options/semi-MDPs organize interaction around temporally extended high-level actions. EP instead separates agent and environment timelines, allowing computation, revision, and multi-rate feedback to be represented as decoupled action–observation events.

Table 2: Hierarchical patrol with interruptions: fixed boundaries at module and state levels introduce response latency, while EP makes routines observable and interruptible, improving alarm handling and performance over loop-based and patched methods.
Table 21: One-dish results by dish family. Cells report mean ± half-width of the bootstrap 95% confidence interval.

Figure 6: Loop unable to interrupt an in-progress checkpoint handling. At tick 12 the agent is handling checkpoint A with no alarm; at tick 13 an alarm appears at C with deadline 20. Because Loop only re-decides at module boundaries, the agent stays in HANDLE_CKPT and the handling counter keeps decrementing (12 → 11 → 10 → 9) while the alarm deadline shrinks in lockstep (19 → 18 → 17). The earliest tick at which Loop could have responded would be after the remaining 9 ticks of handling, by which point the alarm has only 11 ticks left and the agent must still cross the grid to reach C.

Figure 7: Representative episode from the resume_pressure family with three dishes, three tutor problems, and one stove slot. The upper lanes show generated tutor segments for Q1–Q3, while the lower lanes show cooking signals, valid response windows, and finish actions.

Table 25: Evaluation with a 32768-token decoding limit. EP-aware training uses a budgeted reward, while RLVR-8K and RLVR-16K use correctness-only rewards with 8000-token and 16000-token training limits, respectively. We report bootstrap 95% confidence intervals over test examples for Budgeted Success, Accuracy, and Average Tokens.
Table 7: Configuration for the sequential urgency-aware deliberation experiment.
Table 6: Configuration for the single-task urgency-aware deliberation experiment.

Table 3: Digital assistant with asynchronous emails using DeepSeek-V3.2 as the email-triage model. Utility is the total reward under this rescore setup; Balanced is the combined main-task/email score defined above; Timeout is the fraction of emails that miss their deadline; Latency is the average first-response delay to emails; and Main is the normalized main-task completion score.

Figure 2: LLM-based experiments. Tasks can be interpreted as a triage and scheduling problem over a shared timeline. EP exposes ongoing work and incoming events in a way that lets the controller decide whether to interrupt, defer, or continue.

Figure 4: Urgency-conditioned deliberation-mode distributions in the sequential-task setting. EP learns a monotone slack-aware schedule, while the step-trained policy remains biased toward the deepest mode even in tight-deadline states.

Figure 3: Urgency-conditioned deliberation-mode distributions in the single-task setting. The EP-trained policy spreads probability mass across modes according to remaining slack, whereas the step-trained policy heavily favors the deepest mode because deliberation is free during training.

Table 18: Digital assistant with asynchronous emails. Utility, Balanced, Timeout, Latency, and Main are reported as mean ± half-width of bootstrap 95% CI (1000 resamples, seed 42). Utility, Balanced, and Main are higher-is-better; Timeout and Latency are lower-is-better. Each run contains 1000 episodes per method.
Table 17: Environment configuration for the digital assistant with asynchronous emails experiment.

Table 4: Embodied scheduling results using DeepSeek-V3.2. Utility is the total episode score; Dish/Crit. reports dish success in the one-dish setting and critical-dish success in the multi-dish setting. Soft qual. reports soft-dish quality in multi-dish episodes.
Table 24: Tutoring continuity metrics. Cells report mean ± half-width of the bootstrap 95% confidence interval.
Table 10: Decision rules used by the simple hand-written Patch baseline.
Table 12: Configuration for the module-level interruption experiment.
